text: This is a patient with a less severe genotype and phenotype that is appropriate to a total or a near-total colectomy. Historically, the total colectomy and ilorectal anastomosis has a considerable deficit, which is the anastomotic leak rate. Clearly, for a prophylactic operation, it is very high. For this reason, we've described the near total colectomy and iliodistal sigmoid anastomosis, which is a deviation from the current iliorectal anastomotic technique. The principal deviation is that a short length of distal sigmoid is retained and an extracorporeal anastomosis is performed in a side-to-side ileocolic configuration.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of standard laparoscopic ports and initiates the dissection. The left and right middle colic arteries are clearly visible as they are dissected and clips are applied to each artery.

The surgeon uses an energy device to divide the vessels, accessing the lesser sac. Gentle dissection reveals the posterior aspect of the stomach, allowing division of the mesocolon and reaching the right middle colic vessels. The greater omentum is separated from the colon.

The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament, revealing the dissection from below with visible clips. The ileocolic vessels are identified and dissected free using a medial to lateral approach, with clips applied before dividing the vessel.

The surgeon visualizes the duodenum below and performs a full dissection over its surface, reaching the curve between the first and second parts. The anatomical relations are visible, and the surgeon exposes the right hemicolon using a lateral to medial approach.

The surgeon continues the dissection to connect with the previously performed medial to lateral dissection. The right colic artery is visible, with the surgical field achieved by suspending the windows created in the ileocolic region and from the division of the middle colic vessels.

The surgeon frees the descending colon and sigmoid from a lateral to medial approach, mobilizing the colon up to the level of the splenic flexure. The splenic flexure is completely mobilized, and the greater omentum is liberated from the colon. The stomach is visible at the top of the field, providing a clear view of the pancreas. The left hemicolon and mesocolon are visible, with individual vessels identified and divided using an energy device.

The surgeon clips segmental sigmoid arteries and divides them using an energy device. The focus shifts to the right side of the dissection, where the distal ileum is transected to facilitate the extraction of the colon.

The surgeon performs a mini Pfannenstiel incision and inserts a wound protector. The colon is delivered through the incision, and the ileum is exteriorized. Interrupted sutures using 3-0 Vicryl are placed in a side-to-side configuration to stabilize the anastomosis.

Enterotomies are created in both the ileum and colon, and a linear stapling device is inserted. Forceps are used to prevent knots from interfering with the staple line, ensuring the anti-mesenteric borders are stapled. A transverse stapler is then applied across the ileum and colon to complete the side-to-side anastomosis.

The surgeon transfixes any bleeding points with a 3-0 Vicryl suture and buries the staple line using the same suture material. A crotch stitch is placed to stabilize the anastomosis. During re-inspection, the staple line is visible along with the terminal ileum and its loops. The small bowel mesentery is lifted, revealing a straight, non-twisted line tracing back to the duodenum.
Thank you very much for watching.
